1: The dramatic reversal of Isaiah 34's desolation: the wilderness and the dry land will rejoice; the desert will exult and blossom like the crocus. The same creation-vocabulary used for de-creation in Isaiah 34 is now used for re-creation: the dry land (tziyah) and desert (aravah) that were places of desolation are now the subjects of joy and blossoming. The crocus (chavatzelet) is a spring flower -- the image of new growth in the most barren place.
2: Kavod (glory, disp 2) is flagged twice; Elohim and YHWH are flagged -- The glory of Lebanon will be given to it, the splendor of Carmel and Sharon. They will see the glory of the LORD, the splendor of our God. Two occurrences of kavod: first the glory of Lebanon/Carmel/Sharon given to the desert; then the glory of the LORD and the splendor of Elohim seen by the new-creation inhabitants. The MKT's glory is consistent -- the kavod that was Lebanon's is transferred to the wilderness, and the kavod that is the LORD's is then seen directly.
3: Strengthen the weak hands, and steady the shaking knees. The pastoral-exhortation embedded in the eschatological vision: before the final revelation of glory, the discouraged people must be strengthened. Hebrews 12:12 quotes this verse directly: strengthen the feeble arms and weak knees -- applied to the Christian who is running with endurance, encouraged by the example of Jesus who endured the cross for the coming joy.
4: Elohim is flagged twice -- your God will come with vengeance; God will come with divine retribution -- he will come and save you. The three-part divine-coming announcement: (1) with vengeance (naqam), (2) with retribution (gemul Elohim: the recompense of God), (3) to save (yishakhem). The first two are toward enemies; the third is toward God's people. Jesus begins to fulfill this in his healing ministry (vv5-6), while the full vengeance-retribution aspect awaits the second coming.
5: The eyes of the blind opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped -- the two sensory-healing miracles that mark the arrival of the eschatological age. Matthew 11:5 and Luke 7:22 list these exactly when Jesus answers John's question (are you the one who is to come?): the blind receive sight, the deaf hear, the lame walk, the mute speak. The MKT's rendering is close to the LXX form that the NT Gospels cite, which is important for the connection to Jesus' answer to John.
6: The lame leaping like a deer and the mute tongue bursting into song -- the kinetic and vocal miracles. The waters breaking out in the wilderness connects the physical healing to the landscape transformation of v1-2: as the desert blossoms, so the wilderness acquires streams. The healing of the human body and the healing of the land are presented as parallel acts of the same divine restoration. The MKT's burst into song is vivid for the Hebrew teranen: shout/sing with joy.
7: The burning sand becoming a pool and thirsty ground becoming springs -- the inversion of the desert's drought. The haunt of jackals (where only scavengers lived, cf. Isaiah 34:13) becomes a place of grass, reeds, and papyrus. The reed and papyrus are specifically writing-material plants -- the association may be with the book of the LORD (Isaiah 34:16) being fulfilled. The MKT's burning sand for sharav is the correct identification of the desert mirage-sand.
8: Qodesh (holiness, disp 2) is flagged -- a highway will be there, called the Way of Holiness. The Way of Holiness (derech haqodesh) is the consecrated road of the returning exiles. The unclean will not travel on it -- the holy way is protected from defilement. It is for those who walk on that Way; fools will not wander onto it -- the protective exclusivity of the holy highway. The MKT's Way of Holiness is the standard rendering; the highway links back to Isaiah 11:16 (highway from Assyria) and forward to Isaiah 40:3 (prepare the way of the LORD).
9: No lion on the Way -- the predator-free road for the redeemed. The lions absent from the highway contrast with the lions of Isaiah 34:7 (wild oxen alongside Edom's armies) and recall the peaceful coexistence of Isaiah 11:6-9 (lion lying down with the lamb). But the redeemed (geulim) walk there -- they are specifically the ones this road is for. The exclusion of the lion (predator) and the inclusion of the redeemed walker is the shape of the new-creation safety.
10: YHWH is flagged; olam (everlasting, disp 3) is flagged -- the ransomed of the LORD will return; they will come to Zion with singing, with everlasting joy on their heads. The geulim (ransomed/redeemed, from the kinsman-redeemer root goel) come back with singing and simchat olam (everlasting/age-long joy) on their heads. The MKT's everlasting joy renders olam in the absolute sense, not the age-long sense -- the joy is permanent, not merely long-lasting. This verse is quoted in Isaiah 51:11 almost verbatim, making it an Isaianic refrain of the return-promise.
